item.title: Ocean floor seen as a challenge for mapping
item.description: IT’S a truism among oceanographers that there is more accurate mapping of the surface of the moon and Mars than of the deep-ocean floor. That’s especially true for the Bismarck Sea, a relatively deep body of water north of Papua New Guinea.
item.content:encoded:
IT’S a truism among oceanographers that there is more accurate mapping of the surface of the moon and Mars than of the deep-ocean floor.

That’s especially true for the Bismarck Sea, a relatively deep body of water north of Papua New Guinea.

It’s an ocean basin with a geologically complex seafloor rife with faults, volcanic features, rifts, scarps, and active subduction and spreading zones at depths that make high-resolution sonar mapping challenging.

When satellites detected signs of an unexpected submarine volcanic eruption in the Central Bismarck Sea on May 8, 2026, volcanologists were confronted with the reality that no high-resolution maps of the area were available, and relatively little is known about the deep-water eruption setting.

The new eruption is thought to be occurring along the Titan Ridge, about 16km southeast of the location of a submarine eruption in 1972.

However, there is little clarity or consensus among scientists about precisely which volcanic feature may be erupting, the original depth of the currently active vent, or when it last erupted.

Jim Garvin, the chief scientist at National Aeronautics and Space Administration’s (Nasa) Goddard Space Flight Centre, said: “The good news is that there are huge opportunities to explore and learn using both government and commercial satellite platforms already in orbit.”

 What is known is that seismometres detected a small swarm of earthquakes on May 8, followed soon after by clear signs of a submarine eruption in satellite observations.

Beginning on May 9, Nasa’s Aqua and Terra satellites captured optical imagery of white, steam-rich volcanic plumes rising into the atmosphere, while the ocean colour sensor on Nasa’s Pace (Plankton, Aerosol, Cloud, Ocean Ecosystem) satellite revealed discoloured and disturbed water surrounding the eruption site.

On May 12, the VIIRS (Visible Infrared Imaging Radiometer Suite) on Suomi NPP detected thermal anomalies spanning roughly seven square kilometres.

Simon Carn, a volcanologist at Michigan Tech, said: “There must be a lot of hot material near the surface to generate so many thermal anomalies. This suggests a fairly shallow eruption vent – much shallower than what’s implied by the existing bathymetry, which shows water depths of several hundred metres or more.”

Optical satellite imagery shows intense activity in near-surface water, including large plumes of discoloured water and widely distributed steam and ash vents. 

“We’re now eagerly waiting to see if a new island is about to be born – something that we’ve only rarely been able to observe with satellites as it happens,” Garvin said.

If a new island does emerges, volcanologists will be watching it closely to see how it evolves.  – NASA Earth Observatory

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Resource owners must benefit from SEZ: Minister
item.description: INVESTMENT in forest special economic zones (SEZ) in the country must see resource owners sharing direct profits from its arrangements, says Forest minister Salio Waipo. He said with the launch of Diwai Limited Holdings on Friday, as the business arm to the PNG Forest Authority, it was important landowners were educated on their full potential under these special projects.
item.content:encoded:
INVESTMENT in forest special economic zones (SEZ) in the country must see resource owners sharing direct profits from its arrangements, says Forest minister Salio Waipo.

He said with the launch of Diwai Limited Holdings on Friday, as the business arm to the PNG Forest Authority, it was important landowners were educated on their full potential under these special projects.

The establishment of four forest industrial parks or log ponds in Vanimo in West Sepik, Lae in Morobe, Abau in Central and Gazelle in East New Britain, would provide the physical infrastructure and would use existing concessional areas and forest resources, while working to establish a commercial plantation to supply the log pond in the forest industrial park.

Waipo said this was needed to execute the Government’s log export ban and shift the nation’s timber industry from raw log extraction to high-value onshore processing.

“The parks allow Diwai Holdings to partner directly with resource owners and instead of receiving only basic royalties, landowners gain “free-carry” equity in these facilities, directly sharing the profits of the value-added products.

 “These parks will also serve as regional employment hubs, creating thousands of jobs and  establishing heavy manufacturing and milling directly in strategic provinces,” he said.

Waipo said investors would not be left out as the log ponds make it easier for Diwai Holdings to attract international investors who needed reliable, ready-to-use infrastructure such as port access and utilities and to partner in the green economy, timber, and carbon offset initiatives.

Last week, more than 500 participants attended the PNG Forest Sector forum and dual launch of DHL and its four special projects and the national reforestation and afforestation programme 2025 to 2030.

The forum highlighted the need to ensure the government’s policy to ban round log exports to promote domestic downstream processing and retain economic value. This was to be enforced in full by 2025.

Despite the instruction from Prime Minister James Marape, according to advocate group ACT NOW, the log export shipments were still continuing. This drew concern from the participants on how local businesses, resource owners and investors were to qualify and benefit from the incentives to the SEZ.

Waipo said: “The establishment of DHL is timely as PNG round logs for too long have been exported to overseas markets with little value to the resource owners.

“DHL will be seen as the vehicle for the resource owners and PNGFA as regulators, so the benefits will come back to the country and the people,” he said.

He said this transition was not without challenges and was mindful of concerns of industrial players, the expectations of landowners and the broader economic implications.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Concerns over Gulf-Morobe road link
item.description: THE Farmers and Settlers Association says the Gulf-Morobe road link will unlock economic opportunities for both provinces but this potential could stall unless the Wau-Bulolo Highway is fixed first. Association president Wilson Thompson welcomed the breakthrough but said growers still faced the same bottleneck.
item.content:encoded:
THE Farmers and Settlers Association says the Gulf-Morobe road link will unlock economic opportunities for both provinces but this potential could stall unless the Wau-Bulolo Highway is fixed first.

Association president Wilson Thompson welcomed the breakthrough but said growers still faced the same bottleneck.

“The Trans-Island Highway will connect Kaintiba to Kerema and Menyamya, and it will open up many communities,” Thompson said.

The association has pushed for the highway since 2017 with support from Gulf Governor Sir Christopher Haiveta and the late Bulolo MP Sam Basil.

The plan, first mooted by the colonial government in the 1960s, gained momentum after Prime Minister James Marape, Haiveta and Works officials inspected the proposed route in 2019.

Thompson said the highway would also trigger new links from Menyamya to Marawaka, Obura and Kainantu in Eastern Highlands, while Wau-Waria MP Marsh Narewec is pushing for roads from Wau to Biaru, Garaina and Kataipa in Gulf and Goilala in Central.

“All these roads would connect to Garaina valley that has tea and coffee and cocoa but lacks access while we expect more coffee out from Kerema and Obura-Wonenara  to hit the factories and seaports,” Thompson said. But he warned the economic promise would fail unless Government sequenced construction correctly.

“The main feeder road needs to be fixed first, so fuel, machinery and equipment haulage to the three Highway construction sites is easy, cheap and reliable,” Thompson said.

“The immediate work should have been done on the Wau-Bulolo-Menyamya Highway and the Haiveta Highway from Bereina to Kerema. That would provide access to the three Highways of Trans-islands, Sam Basil and Kukukuku Highway that are in construction.”

He added that farmers in Kaintiba, Marawaka, Menyamya, Wau-Waria and Bulolo were crippled by freight costs.

Thompson said a grower pays K80 to K200 to move produce 150km from Wau and Menyamya to Lae, while a farmer in Goroka pays just K50 for 300km.

“A person from Goroka travels 300km to Lae with K50 while a farmer in Menyamya and Wau pays K80 and K200 for half the distance,” he said.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: PNG Power executives visit Morobe to tackle Wau-Bulolo blackouts
item.description: RESIDENTS and businesses in Bulolo and Wau-Waria in Morobe will soon see more reliable electricity services following a high-level PNG Power visit focused on addressing ongoing power disruptions in the area. A senior management team from PNG Power recently completed a three-day inspection tour across Bulolo and Wau-Waria districts to assess power challenges affecting local communities, businesses
item.content:encoded:
RESIDENTS and businesses in Bulolo and Wau-Waria in Morobe will soon see more reliable electricity services following a high-level PNG Power visit focused on addressing ongoing power disruptions in the area.

A senior management team from PNG Power recently completed a three-day inspection tour across Bulolo and Wau-Waria districts to assess power challenges affecting local communities, businesses and industries.

The delegation was led by PNG Power chief executive officer Paul Bayly alongside chief technical officer Tony Koiri and Ramu Grid Momase regional manager Albert Nanako.

During the visit, the team met local workers, community leaders and business operators who have been affected by frequent outages and unstable electricity supply.

In Wau, the executives inspected areas experiencing regular weekly blackouts and discussed possible solutions with local stakeholders.

Communities in the district have long depended on the Bulolo power system and hydropower generation from PNG Forest Products.

The delegation also visited Hidden Valley mine to discuss alternative power supply options and inspected possible hydropower sites at the Kaisinik village area and along Kaindi road as part of future renewable energy plans for the district.

Wau centre manager Morgan Legra thanked the PNG Power executives for visiting the district and listening to workers and residents.

“Having senior management come to the ground gives confidence to our staff and communities that the power issues affecting the district are finally being prioritised,” Legra said.

Bayly said PPL remained committed to improving electricity reliability and strengthening support for local operations in remote districts.

The visit also included the announcement of four new apprentices to assist Wau-based linemen and improve technical capacity in the area.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: AJ in town. Johnston jets in for short visit
item.description: RUGBY league fans in Port Moresby will today have the chance to meet NRL’s all-time leading try scorer, Alex Johnston, who is scheduled to arrive at noon for a short but special visit. The South Sydney Rabbitohs winger will headline a public meet-and-greet tomorrow (Tuesday) at the Range View Plaza, an event expected to draw large crowds, particularly young supporters eager to meet one of Papua Ne
item.content:encoded:
RUGBY league fans in Port Moresby will today have the chance to meet NRL’s all-time leading try scorer, Alex Johnston, who is scheduled to arrive at noon for a short but special visit.

The South Sydney Rabbitohs winger will headline a public meet-and-greet tomorrow (Tuesday) at the Range View Plaza, an event expected to draw large crowds, particularly young supporters eager to meet one of Papua New Guinea’s most-celebrated rugby league representatives.

Organised with support from Lae Biscuit Company Ltd, the programme will feature fan activities, media appearances, photo opportunities, and promotional shoots.

Customer service manager Roy Cruz confirmed Johnston will return to Australia on Wednesday, but noted that his brief stay will be packed with engagements.

“The programme starts from 10.30am, while the official meet-and-greet with fans will begin at 1.30pm,” Cruz said.

He added that the morning schedule will focus on production work, including photos and video sessions, before Johnston connects with supporters later in the day.

Johnston’s visit comes at a pivotal moment for rugby league in PNG, following his recent inclusion in the inaugural PNG Chiefs squad alongside West Tigers playmaker Jarome Luai. His presence is expected to fuel excitement around the new franchise as preparations continue for its historic NRL entry in 2028.

For fans, the return of Kumul #294 is more than just a celebrity appearance. Johnston remains a proud symbol of PNG rugby league, with his NRL achievements continuing to inspire the next generation of players across the country.

According to Cruz, Johnston’s involvement also represents a significant boost for the PNG Chiefs brand, strengthening public support and building momentum ahead of the club’s journey toward NRL competition in 2028.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Teen golfer leaves for tourney
item.description: FIFTEEN-year-old Jabez Ireng from Koropak village on Karkar Island, Madang, made his first international appearance for Papua New Guinea at the Asia-Pacific Amateur Championship Academy and Tournament in Auckland, New Zealand. The tournament started on Friday through to May 30.
item.content:encoded:
FIFTEEN-year-old Jabez Ireng from Koropak village on Karkar Island, Madang, made his first international appearance for Papua New Guinea at the Asia-Pacific Amateur Championship Academy and Tournament in Auckland, New Zealand.

The tournament started on Friday through to May 30.

Ireng left Port Moresby on Friday for Auckland.

A Grade 9 student at Tusbab Secondary School and member of the Madang Country Club, Ireng was invited to the regional event by Augusta National Golf Club of the United States, with endorsement from the PNG Golf Association.

The Asia-Pacific Amateur Championship (AAC) brings together the region’s top amateur golfers and is recognised as a pathway to global competition.

Ireng will participate in both the AAC Academy and Tournament at Te Arai Links, Auckland.

The 15-year-old began playing golf at age eight at the Madang Country Club. His dedication has seen him rise quickly through the junior ranks.

Last year, he finished as the Madang and PNG junior champions.

Ireng’s success at the national level has earned him the opportunity to represent PNG internationally for the first time.

“This is a proud moment for Jabez, his family, Madang Country Club, and PNG Golf,” a spokesperson for the PNG Golf Association said. “At 15, he’s showing the discipline and talent needed to compete at the regional level.

“We thank Augusta National Golf Club for the invitation and look forward to seeing Jabez gain valuable experience in Auckland.”

His participation is expected to inspire other junior golfers across Papua New Guinea. PNG Golf Association president Ian Hodges also wished Jabez his best performances at the Te Arai Links.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Aero Medics dominate Round 7
item.description: AERO Medics opened the premier men’s division Round 7 clashes with a commanding 2 0 win over H20, scoring 25 21 and 25 20 on Saturday at the PNG Education Institute (PNGEI) courts. The Volleyball Fairfax League delivered thrilling encounters across all divisions, with standout performances and nail biting finishes that kept fans on edge.
item.content:encoded:
AERO Medics opened the premier men’s division Round 7 clashes with a commanding 2 0 win over H20, scoring 25 21 and 25 20 on Saturday at the PNG Education Institute (PNGEI) courts.

The Volleyball Fairfax League delivered thrilling encounters across all divisions, with standout performances and nail biting finishes that kept fans on edge.

Aero Medics captain Raycoh Kerue praised his teammates, highlighting the setter’s control of the game as the key to their success.

Kerue, a longtime participant in Fairfax competitions, now leads a Badili based team composed of his brothers in the men’s division and his sisters in the women’s division, who are competing in their first season.

“We currently have five teams in the competition – three men’s and two women’s. We aim to involve more youths so they have something exciting to look forward to on weekends instead of just hanging around the community causing problems,” Kerue explained.

Other men’s division highlights included:

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: PETE’S PUNCHLINE
item.description: Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.
item.content:encoded: Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Collective effort in elections
item.description: PAPUA New Guinea is approaching General Election 2027, a crucial moment for democracy, national unity, and future development. This election will test not only political leadership, but also the shared responsibility of citizens and institutions to uphold peace, fairness, and the rule of law.
item.content:encoded:
PAPUA New Guinea is approaching General Election 2027, a crucial moment for democracy, national unity, and future development.

This election will test not only political leadership, but also the shared responsibility of citizens and institutions to uphold peace, fairness, and the rule of law.

A free and fair election cannot be achieved by government alone.

It requires collective effort from individuals, families, students, communities, political actors, and state institutions working in the national interest.

Citizens must treat voting as a civic duty, rejecting vote-buying, tribal pressure, misinformation, and violence, and instead choose leaders based on integrity, competence, and vision.

Young people must be strengthened through civic education to understand democratic values and resist manipulation, particularly on social media.

Families also play a vital role in promoting peace, discouraging tribal conflict and violence, and building strong values at home.

Community leaders, churches, women’s groups, and youth organisations should promote voter awareness, peacebuilding, and conflict resolution, while discouraging weapons and division.

Political parties and candidates must prioritise integrity and policy over money, commit to peaceful campaigning, and accept results responsibly.

State institutions, including the Electoral Commission, police, defence, and Correctional Services, must ensure fairness and security throughout the electoral process.

The success of the election depends on collective responsibility and a national commitment to peace, integrity, and democracy.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Time to breakthe silence
item.description: IT is deeply concerning that family violence and sexual harassment are increasingly reported in mainstream and social media. Shockingly, many perpetrators are family members – those we should trust for comfort and protection, including fathers, grandparents, uncles, and cousins – committing immoral acts against vulnerable children.
item.content:encoded:
IT is deeply concerning that family violence and sexual harassment are increasingly reported in mainstream and social media.

Shockingly, many perpetrators are family members – those we should trust for comfort and protection, including fathers, grandparents, uncles, and cousins – committing immoral acts against vulnerable children.

Even more troubling is that many of these crimes go unreported.

Victims often suffer in silence, trapped by trauma and fear of retaliation.

Families and communities may be aware, yet the perpetrators continue their actions seemingly unchallenged, while the victims bear the consequences alone.

When will we break the silence and confront the evil eroding our families and society?

Remaining passive allows these acts to destroy human dignity and values.

We must not ignore issues that demand lawful and prompt intervention.

Evil thrives when those entrusted with authority and moral responsibility do nothing.

Failure to act now endangers the current generation and sets a dangerous precedent for the future.

It is our collective duty to protect the vulnerable, uphold justice, and ensure that families and communities are safe from harm.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Gaps in disaster preparedness
item.description: THERE are reports from various sources of possible underwater volcanic activity in the Bismarck Sea near Manus, with concerns raised about smoke or disturbance in the ocean and the potential risk of a tsunami. While these reports remain unverified, they have caused confusion and anxiety among local communities.
item.content:encoded:
THERE are reports from various sources of possible underwater volcanic activity in the Bismarck Sea near Manus, with concerns raised about smoke or disturbance in the ocean and the potential risk of a tsunami.

While these reports remain unverified, they have caused confusion and anxiety among local communities.

Manus residents have raised concerns on social media, noting that some islanders lack boats or safe means of evacuation to the mainland and are uncertain about receiving timely and accurate updates.

This has highlighted gaps in real-time communication and disaster preparedness.

At the same time, there are questions about the absence of clear official communication from responsible authorities, including the National Disaster Centre and government leaders.

Some citizens are calling for stronger coordination between national and provincial agencies to ensure timely warnings, evacuation plans, and clear public information during potential emergencies.

The situation has raised broader concerns about disaster response readiness in Papua New Guinea, particularly for remote island communities.

It underscores the importance of effective communication systems, preparedness planning, and rapid response mechanisms to protect lives in the event of natural disasters.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: World-class sports deserve better funding
item.description: PAPUA New Guinea is a nation passionate about sport, yet national funding remains heavily skewed toward one code while other internationally successful sports are overlooked. The SP PNG Hunters inspire pride, but they compete in a state-level Australian competition.
item.content:encoded:
PAPUA New Guinea is a nation passionate about sport, yet national funding remains heavily skewed toward one code while other internationally successful sports are overlooked.

The SP PNG Hunters inspire pride, but they compete in a state-level Australian competition.

In contrast, the PNG Mosquitoes (AFL) are genuine world champions, winning three International Cup titles, reaching six consecutive world grand finals, and securing the 2024 Pacific Cup.

They are arguably the most dominant international Australian Rules team outside Australia.

The PNG Barramundis have also excelled globally.

As reigning ICC East Asia-Pacific champions, they regularly qualify for T20 World Cups and place PNG alongside major cricketing nations.

Despite these achievements, AFL and cricket continue to operate on limited budgets, lacking adequate facilities, travel support, and grassroots investment.

If the PNG Sports Foundation and corporate sponsors direct even a small portion of funding into these sports, PNG could become a true multi-sport powerhouse.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Pacific concerns about militarisation and NZ’s role in it
item.description: NEW Zealand’s government is increasingly eager to promote the buy-in of Pacific nations for closer defence force integration in the region, amid concerns about militarisation of the region. The security environment has been shifting rapidly, and regional defence is becoming more complex, leaving Pacific Islanders wondering if their “Ocean of Peace” is slipping out of their grasp.
item.content:encoded:
NEW Zealand’s government is increasingly eager to promote the buy-in of Pacific nations for closer defence force integration in the region, amid concerns about militarisation of the region.

The security environment has been shifting rapidly, and regional defence is becoming more complex, leaving Pacific Islanders wondering if their “Ocean of Peace” is slipping out of their grasp.

In recent months, the defence and police forces of Australia and New Zealand have been increasing cooperation with counterparts in Pacific countries, including Fiji, Tonga, Samoa and Vanuatu in efforts to combat transnational crime, especially the illicit drug trade. But as a number of Pacific Island countries weigh up signing major bilateral treaties or agreements with the likes of Australia, China and the United States (US), New Zealand has been steadily pushing Pacific regional defence cooperation on a number of fronts.

The communiqué from last October’s South Pacific Defence Ministers Meeting (SPDMM) in Chile is instructive.

The SPDMM – which involves New Zealand, Australia, Chile, Fiji, France, Papua New Guinea and Tonga – notes the leadership role New Zealand has taken on better coordinating regional defence architecture.

The member countries agreed to push for a defence advisor from their collective to be embedded in the secretariat of the Pacific Islands Forum (PIF), whose secretary-general Baron Waqa attended the Chile meeting and appeared to support closer integration.

While the advisor position is yet to be established, the SPDMM is surging ahead with a range of new regional defence initiatives, including developing the Pacific Response Group, under which defence personnel from Australia, Fiji, France, New Zealand and PNG work together to support coordinated humanitarian assistance and disaster relief responses across the Pacific.

A briefing from March’s joint meeting of the defence and foreign ministers of Australia and New Zealand, emphasised how they aim to promote “the sense of integration through Pacific defence forces’ and to ‘enhance the sense of Pacific forces meeting Pacific security needs”.

It also highlighted a keenness to “get more links between SPDMM and PIF so that these voices are heard directly by the region”.

“The briefings reveal New Zealand’s role in integrating and aligning Pacific defence forces alongside a considerable anxiety about regional social licence,” Pacific historian Marco de Jong said.

He said the language being used “speaks to a programme of influence and public relations, calibrated to downplay criticism that New Zealand is contributing to the militarisation of the Pacific”.

A representative with Development Alternatives with Women for a New Era, Maureen Penjueli, who is also a long time advocate for indigenous rights in the region, said there had been a lack of consultation with the wider Pacific Islands region about the new defence tack.

“We’ve seen so much occupation by those in the defence interests area. For example, the Australian National Security College takes a very primary seat at the Pacific Islands Forum on security. We’ve got competing interests, which is the Fusion Centre that’s in Vanuatu,” Penjueli said.

“When you add more and more players to this regional architecture that already has enough players on defence and security, it complicates the governance structure in a way.

“It does not go through the rigour of national consultations, consultations with civil society around some of these bigger significant shifts around defence and security.”

New Zealand is a long-standing contributor to Pacific regional initiatives, and its defence force is well valued in the region, especially in responding to disasters, humanitarian needs, transnational crime and maritime security threats, and also including in training support.

New Zealand’s defence minister Chris Penk, who replaced Judith Collins in the role since March’s 2+2 Ministerial Meeting with Australia, said that New Zealand always sought to adapt its work with Pacific partners to their context, culture and operational needs.

He told RNZ that in order to support closer cooperation between Pacific militaries, members were also looking at a SPDMM Status of Forces Agreement.

“This would provide a common legal framework for personnel to deploy into each other’s countries more easily, strengthening our collective ability to respond to maritime security challenges as well as humanitarian and disaster relief events.”

Asked if New Zealand is contributing to militarisation of the Pacific Islands, Penk said Pacific partners had warmly welcomed the country’s continued presence and partnership in the region

“The New Zealand defence force contributes to regional responses where it is agreed that defence force personnel and assets should be involved, including humanitarian assistance, maritime domain awareness, fisheries patrols, and search and rescue operations.

“As a Pacific military, we are proud to work alongside our Pacific partners to help respond to the challenges facing our region.”

Penjueli warned that militarisation of the region was escalating against the wishes of most Pacific Islands people.

Making things more complicated, she said, was the growing number of security treaties and agreements that Island countries were being drawn to.

She said they were no longer just about defence or security inter-operability, and often included development and economic dimensions, arrangements that “entangled” Pacific countries into wide ranging commitments beyond traditional military and security ties.

Penjueli worried that the interests of the Islands countries themselves were more than ever being buried under broader geopolitical jostling.

“We were told that this is to ready the region in an anticipation, to contain China, and we’re told that this is about the drug trade and the drug war that’s taking place.

“Yet for the Pacific, climate change or the climate crisis, remains our significant issue around security. So, I think the agendas are very different.”

At their last leaders summit, PIF countries signed up to the Blue Pacific Ocean of Peace Declaration, formally committing the region to peace, sovereignty, and climate justice.

However, Penjueli said being a true ocean of peace required demilitarisation and de-escalation – something which she suggested is not the direction that the defence-oriented governments of the region are heading in. – RNZ

item.title: Ex-security guard Zeming graduates with diploma
item.description: THANKS to young sister Penelope who had challenged family members to pursue further studies and graduate from tertiary institutions, Zeming Zeming received not only a Diploma in Business Management but also the top-student award at the International Training Institute graduation on Friday. “In my immediate family, only the two of us have graduated – me and my small sister (Penelope). She had
item.content:encoded:
THANKS to young sister Penelope who had challenged family members to pursue further studies and graduate from tertiary institutions, Zeming Zeming received not only a Diploma in Business Management but also the top-student award at the International Training Institute graduation on Friday.

“In my immediate family, only the two of us have graduated – me and my small sister (Penelope). She had graduated earlier (in 2022) and she used to challenge us to obtain qualifications (from tertiary institutions) as well.”

Zeming, 31, of Zankoa village in Tewae-Siassi district, Morobe used to score good marks in primary school but never became a top student or dux.

“I was never a dux. This is my first time being a dux. I thank God and my family for this.”

Zeming is the third eldest of five siblings – two brothers and three sisters. Their father Mao Zeming is self-employed while Mum Fredah died in 2018.

Zeming did not complete school because of financial issues. So he took on informal jobs to enroll in the diploma course at ITI.

“I didn’t complete my schooling due to school fee issues. I looked for jobs instead. I became a bus driver, security guard and all sorts of informal jobs until this opportunity arose and I grabbed it with both hands.”

His job as a security guard kindled his interest in management.

“I worked as a security and was happy to interact with people. I felt like I could manage their small complaints and issues. It got me interested in the management course.”

He faced challenges during the two years of study.

“The first two years were not easy. I tried my best to go to work and attend classes. And in the end, I achieved this.”

His family motivated him to get his diploma.

“We are a close-knit family. Every time my brother would counsel me. He reminded me of our humble beginnings and not to take education for granted. It was their motivation that kept me going.”

His father’s strict rules on time guided his study routine.

“ I didn’t complete my schooling due to school fee issues. I looked for jobs instead. I became a bus driver, security guard and all sorts of informal jobs until this opportunity arose and I grabbed it with both hands.”

“Focus is one thing that my father talks about every time, reminding us to be early before schedule and all that. He is a very strict man, very strict on time.

“Some things we don’t know about, he encourages us to ask people for help, to be curious. So yes, it was my father’s strictness that had shaped my study routine.”

“I always make sure that I did my homework and my studies and that things are in order.

“The good thing is that, I have a good family. They are very understanding people. Once in a while they would ask me for help. But mostly, they respect me; they played their part and mine as well.”

“My brother is always my role model. He used to remind us that success is there, all you need to do to achieve it is to remain focused.

“He does not have a big job but he ensures that we all are taken care of. He took on the family responsibility and I have respect for him.”

Zeming is happy to have accomplished his goal.

“I am really happy. Seeing (cousin sister) Luddy also graduating today makes me really happy. We helped each other at school. We also help other Morobe students as well and in the end, we made it.”

Zeming dedicated the dux award to his late mother and his diploma to his father.

“I dedicated these achievements to my family, those who had helped me with my school fees and my upbringing. And this dux award is dedicated to my late mum. Dad gets the diploma.”

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Neidab dedicates degree, award to late father
item.description: WHEN Neidab Ulu went on stage to receive his degree and award, he was oblivious to the cheering, applause and claps as he was missing and thinking of the person he wanted to be there to witness his achievement – his late father. “I lost my father during the first semester last year. He should have been attending and witnessing this ceremony.”
item.content:encoded:
WHEN Neidab Ulu went on stage to receive his degree and award, he was oblivious to the cheering, applause and claps as he was missing and thinking of the person he wanted to be there to witness his achievement – his late father.

“I lost my father during the first semester last year. He should have been attending and witnessing this ceremony.”

Neidab lost his father Ulu Rusini last year during the second semester of his final year – a critical time for any final year university student.

However, he managed to pull through and graduated with a Bachelor in Public Finance and Accountancy degree.

Neidab, 39, from Wanigela Village, Abau District, Central, is the second eldest of six children.

He was among those who graduated from the University of PNG School of Business and Public Policy last Thursday.

To his employer, the Public Service Commission, he was an investment which paid off when he not only graduated but also named the top performing student in the Bachelor of Public Finance and Accountancy class.

He received the Academic Excellence Award after being sponsored by the PSC, where he has worked for 11 years, to pursue the degree while working as the acting Senior Budget Officer and Certifying Officer.

It was an opportunity he took seriously.

“I thank my employer, the Public Service Commission, for the investment and opportunity and trust placed in me to send me for further studies.”

His work involves budget support, expenditure management, and financial advisory duties. He knows that the degree will help him in his work and lift his performance.

“What motivated me to go back for further studies was to enhance my skills in my current duties on the processes in place to manage the public funds effectively and efficiently.”

Neidab attained a Certificate in Business Studies in 2007 and a Diploma in Business Studies in 2008 at the Port Moresby Business College.

In 2023, he graduated with a Diploma in Accounting from the Somare Institute of Leadership and Governance (SILAG).

The loss of his father affected him. On top of that, he faced financial issues and tried his best to juggle his time between work, study, and family.

“I encountered financial challenges attending classes, attending to family matters, as well as work.”

At times when work and study demanded his attention most, he prioritised which one to tackle first.

“I’ve had clashes between my studies and my job. Some time, I had to attend to my work first and then my studies second because work is my bread and butter.”

The experience changed how he managed his time.

“It shaped me as a person to think critically and handle issues in a way that is balanced.”

At home, his wife Wendy Ulu and two sons Neidrolson who is in Grade Six, and Braden in Grade 4, endured nights when he had to burn the midnight oil.

Wife Wendy remembers those nights when they hardly went to sleep as Neidab would spend hours studying and keeping the lights on.

So she was happy and relieved to see him graduate with flying colours – a fitting reward for his hard work and perseverance. For the family, the hours of struggling to sleep are finally over.

Neidab dedicated the award to his employer, his late father, and his family.

“I dedicate the award to my employer and to my father who should have attended and witnessed this ceremony. And not forgetting my family who supported me throughout this journey.”

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.
